title: Managing Secrets in CI/CD Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on securely managing AWS keys, variables, and OIDC in GitHub.

questions.question: In GitHub Actions, what prevents a developer from reading a production database password embedded in the repository settings?

options.text: The values are permanently encrypted and hidden from the UI immediately after they are saved; they can only be deleted or overwritten.
The values are hashed using MD5.
They must enter a 2FA code every time they load the settings page.
AWS blocks access to GitHub API queries automatically.

questions.question: What is the correct syntax for referencing a stored repository secret inside a GitHub Actions `.yml` workflow file?

options.text: ${SECRET_NAME}
%%secrets.NAME%%
${{ secrets.NAME }}
@[NAME]

questions.question: What does the GitHub Actions runner automatically do if a script attempts to `echo` or print an active Secret to the console output?

options.text: It bans the repository from executing workflows.
It intercepts the output and redacts the value, replacing the secret string with '***' in the visible logs.
It deletes the secret entirely from settings.
It emails the repository admin immediately.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the most secure, modern industry standard for authenticating GitHub Actions directly to an AWS Cloud Environment without storing static access keys?

options.text: Hardcoding the keys into a private Git branch.
OpenID Connect (OIDC) Federation, utilizing temporary, short-lived session tokens.
Storing the keys in AWS Secrets Manager and making the bucket public temporarily.
Using the AWS Root Account credentials.

questions.question: Why are static, long-lived AWS IAM Access Keys stored as Secrets considered an 'anti-pattern' compared to OIDC?

options.text: Because they cost extra money.
They expire every 15 minutes, causing builds to fail randomly.
Because they reduce execution speed significantly.
Because if stolen, they do not automatically expire, remaining valid indefinitely until a human manually rotates or deletes them.
